attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department by ambulance with right shoulder pain following a motor-vehicle roll over. He was travelling 40km/hr when a kangaroo appeared out of nowhere. The patient mentioned that he fell on his right arm and subsequently injured his right shoulder. His shoulder pain is constant and severe with 8/10 in intensity.

On examination, his right arm is abducted and externally rotated. There is loss of the normal contour of the deltoid with obvious bruises around the right arm. Passive and active movement of the right shoulder is limited due to pain. A plain x-ray of the right shoulder is taken (Image)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of anterior shoulder dislocation supported by his clinical presentation of acute shoulder pain in the background of trauma, his right arm which is slightly abducted and externally rotated, and the restricted passive and active movement of the right shoulder. With any shoulder dislocation, neurovascular examination should be done as the brachial plexus and brachial artery are at risk. Axillary nerve is the most common nerve to be injured from an anterior shoulder dislocation. Other complications from anterior shoulder dislocation include Bony defects (Hill sachs lesion / Bankart injury) and tearing of the rotator cuff muscles.

Patients with suspected shoulder dislocation should receive an AP, scapular "Y" view, and an axillary view shoulder x-ray. In his case, the film demonstrates a dislocated humeral head lying on the subcoracoid position, confirming the diagnosis of anterior shoulder dislocation. This is accompanied by a transverse fracture of the distal 2/3 of the right clavicle with minimal displacement. An urgent referral to the orthopaedic team should be made for further management and shoulder reduction.

Inferior shoulder dislocations account for <1% of all shoulder dislocations. They are a hyperabduction injury and the patient typically presents with a "hand on their head" posture (abducted arm and flexed elbow). Anterior shoulder dislocations (95%) and posterior shoulder dislocations (5%) are more common.

Posterior shoulder dislocation presents with adduction of the arm with slight internal rotation. This is accompanied with inability of the arm to perform external rotation. Imaging commonly show the "light bulb" sign due to the internally rotated humeral head.

Humeral neck fracture is not the correct diagnosis as there is no sign of humeral neck fracture on the x-ray.

There are no signs of acromioclavicular (AC) joint dislocation, making the diagnosis unlikely.
learningPoints: Axillary nerve is the most common nerve to be injured from an anterior shoulder dislocation.
Patients with anterior shoulder dislocation present with abduction and external rotation of the affected arm.
references: Cutts S, Prempeh M, Drew S. Anterior shoulder dislocation. The Annals of The Royal College of Surgeons of England. 2009;91(1):2-7. Available at: https://read.qxmd.com/read/19126329/anterior-shoulder-dislocation

Sherman SC, Wolfson AB, Grayzel J. Shoulder dislocation and reduction. In: UpToDate. 2016; cited 2022 Jul. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/shoulder-dislocation-and-reduction?source=search_result&search=shoulder%20dislocation&selectedTitle=1~39
